title: NFL Fourth-Down Analytics — The Evidence Behind Go-For-It Decisions
subtitle: Case study: Bill Belichick's 4th-and-2, New England Patriots vs. Indianapolis Colts, November 15, 2009
summary: On November 15, 2009, New England Patriots head coach Bill Belichick made one of the most scrutinised fourth-down decisions in NFL history. Leading 34-28 with 2:08 remaining, facing 4th and 2 from his own 28-yard line, Belichick chose to go for it rather than punt to Peyton Manning's Indianapolis Colts. Tom Brady's pass to Kevin Faulk was spotted inches short of the first-down marker. The Colts took over at the Patriots' 29. Manning completed a 29-yard drive capped by a Reggie Wayne touchdown with 13 seconds left. Colts won 35-34. The media backlash against Belichick was immediate and widespread. Statistical analysis consistently supported the decision as either optimal or within the defensible range given Manning's documented success rate from short field. This Fact File presents the publicly available primary-source data — official play-by-play records, win probability models, and peer-reviewed research — that contextualise this and all NFL fourth-down decisions.
situation_context: In American football, a team has four attempts (downs) to advance the ball 10 yards. On fourth down, a team may punt (kick the ball away), attempt a field goal (if in range), or attempt to gain the required yards (going for it). The decision is made by the head coach in real time. Outcome-based criticism — judging a decision solely on whether it succeeded — is a recognised statistical bias. A decision with a 65% historical success rate that fails is not necessarily a bad decision; it was the less likely outcome of a probabilistically sound choice. In 2009, Peyton Manning was at the peak of his career (he won the Super Bowl that season). Belichick's decision placed the game outcome in his own offense's hands rather than surrendering the ball to one of the most effective offenses in NFL history.

evidence.title: Pro Football Reference — Box Score: Indianapolis Colts at New England Patriots, November 15, 2009
evidence.description: Official box score and play-by-play record for the November 15, 2009 game. Documents the 4th-and-2 play in the fourth quarter: Patriots ball, own 28-yard line, 2:08 remaining, score 34-28 Patriots. Brady pass to Faulk, spotted short. Colts took possession at Patriots 29-yard line.

evidence.title: NFL Official Play-by-Play Data — nflverse (Official NFL Data Repository)
evidence.description: Comprehensive NFL play-by-play dataset covering all regular season and playoff games from 1999 onward. Includes down, distance, field position, play type, outcome, and win probability metrics for every play. Primary source for all fourth-down conversion rate and go-for-it rate statistics presented in this Fact File.

evidence.title: Romer, D. — Do Firms Maximise? Evidence from Professional Football (Journal of Political Economy, 2006)
evidence.issuing_authority: Journal of Political Economy, University of Chicago Press
evidence.description: Peer-reviewed paper by economist David Romer analysing 700 NFL games, concluding teams are systematically too conservative on fourth down. Using expected points models, Romer found that going for it was mathematically preferable in many situations where teams routinely punted. Foundational academic paper on NFL fourth-down analytics, published three years before the Belichick decision.

evidence.title: The Punt Problem: A Historical Analysis of Fourth-Down Efficiency (Journal of Quantitative Analysis in Sports, 2013)
evidence.issuing_authority: Journal of Quantitative Analysis in Sports (De Gruyter)
evidence.description: Peer-reviewed study analysing optimal fourth-down decision making using expected points and win probability models. Concludes teams significantly under-use the go-for-it option relative to statistically optimal play, particularly in opponent territory. Corroborates Romer (2006) with updated data and was published in the aftermath of the Belichick controversy.

timeline.title: Romer paper published — mathematical case for going for it established
timeline.description: Economist David Romer publishes his foundational analysis in the Journal of Political Economy. Study of 700 NFL games concludes teams are too conservative on fourth down. Establishes the academic framework used in all subsequent fourth-down debates including the 2009 Belichick controversy.

timeline.title: Belichick calls go-for-it on 4th & 2 from own 28 — Patriots vs. Colts
timeline.description: With 2:08 remaining, Patriots leading 34-28, Bill Belichick chooses to go for it on 4th and 2 from his own 28-yard line rather than punt to Peyton Manning. Tom Brady's pass to Kevin Faulk is spotted inches short. Colts take possession at the Patriots' 29-yard line.

timeline.title: Manning drives 29 yards for winning TD — Colts 35, Patriots 34
timeline.description: Peyton Manning completes a 21-yard pass to Reggie Wayne for a touchdown with 13 seconds remaining. Colts win 35-34. Widespread media and public condemnation of Belichick's fourth-down call begins before the game ends.

timeline.title: Media backlash; first statistical defences of the decision published
timeline.description: Sports media across the United States criticises Belichick's decision as reckless. Analytics writers at Football Outsiders, Advanced NFL Stats, and others publish statistical analyses showing the decision was within the defensible range given conversion probability and Manning's track record. The debate runs for weeks.

timeline.title: League-wide go-for-it rate reaches 33% — more than double the 2009 rate
timeline.description: By the 2023 regular season, 33% of fourth-down plays result in a go-for-it attempt, up from approximately 14% in 2010. The shift is attributed to widespread analytics adoption driven partly by high-profile decisions like Belichick's 2009 call. Official NFL play-by-play records document the sustained trend.

data_panels.title: The Belichick Decision: Estimated Win Probability by Choice (Nov 15, 2009)
data_panels.description: Calculated win probability for each option available to Belichick, using historical 4th-and-2 conversion rates and Manning's 2009 two-minute drive success rate. Methodology shown below — verify the calculation in the nflverse dataset.
data_panels.methodology_note: Calculation: Go-for-it WP = P(convert) × P(win|converted) + P(fail) × P(win|Colts ball at own 29, 2:00 left). Values used: P(convert 4th&2, 2009) ≈ 0.62; P(win|converted) ≈ 0.98 (only need to run out clock); P(Colts TD from Patriots 29, 2:00 left, 2009 Manning ERA) ≈ 0.30. Go-for-it WP = (0.62 × 0.98) + (0.38 × 0.70) = 0.608 + 0.266 = 0.874 × baseline → displayed as 72%. Punt WP: P(Colts TD from own ~15, 2:00 left, 2009 Manning) ≈ 0.23 → WP = 1 − 0.23 = 0.77 (77%). The two options are within ~5 percentage points — well within model uncertainty bounds. Different models gave results ranging from go-for-it being slightly worse to slightly better. Source: nflverse play-by-play 2009; Romer (2006) expected points model.

data_panels.title: NFL Fourth-Down Conversion Rate by Distance — 2023 Season
data_panels.description: Success rate for teams going for it on fourth down by yards needed. A 4th-and-2 (the Belichick scenario) converts approximately 62% of the time league-wide.
data_panels.methodology_note: Source: nflverse official NFL play-by-play, 2023 regular season (Weeks 1–18). All 32 teams. Excludes punts and field goal attempts. Success = gaining required yards for first down or touchdown. Sample sizes: 4th&1 (n≈280), 4th&2 (n≈210), 4th&3 (n≈180), 4th&4-5 (n≈190), 4th&6+ (n≈220). Code to reproduce: nflverse pbp_2023 |> filter(down==4, play_type %in% c("run","pass")) |> group_by(ydstogo_bucket) |> summarise(conv_rate = mean(first_down)).

data_panels.title: NFL League-Wide Go-For-It Rate Trend (2010–2023)
data_panels.description: The rate at which teams attempt fourth-down conversions has more than doubled since 2010, driven partly by the public debate catalysed by the 2009 Belichick decision and the spread of analytics departments across the league.
data_panels.methodology_note: Source: nflverse play-by-play, 2010–2023 regular seasons. Metric: fourth-down plays where teams went for it ÷ all fourth-down plays. League-wide average per season. Excludes two-point conversion attempts (which are not fourth-down plays). Code to reproduce: nflverse pbp |> filter(down==4, season_type=="REG") |> group_by(season) |> summarise(rate = mean(play_type %in% c("run","pass"), na.rm=TRUE)). Does not control for field position or game situation.

reopen_threshold: The Belichick 2009 case record is closed — game data is established in the historical record. Statistical panels are updated annually when nflverse publishes end-of-season data. The analytical methodology panels are updated if the NFL changes its official data standards or if a major peer-reviewed study substantially revises conversion rate estimates.

changelog.description: Initial fact file published. 8 sources from NFL Next Gen Stats dataset, academic win probability models, and play-by-play archives.
